summary:
Evidence for elaborate attention to the dead and possible ancestor veneration is widespread across Neolithic communities of the Near East during the eighth and seventh millennia BCE. The most striking evidence comes from the practice of plastered skulls — found at sites including Jericho, [[event:26|Nahal Hemar]], and related southern Levantine sites — where the skulls of the dead were carefully modeled with plaster to reconstruct facial features, sometimes inlaid with shell for eyes, and apparently kept among the living as meaningful objects rather than simply buried. This practice suggests that the identity and presence of the dead continued to matter to survivors in ways that went beyond simply disposing of remains.

At [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]], the practice of burying the dead beneath the floors of houses where families continued to live reflects a similar pattern: the dead were kept spatially close to the living, suggesting ongoing relationships between the living and deceased members of the community. Some burials at Çatalhöyük show evidence of secondary treatment — bodies were sometimes retrieved, defleshed, and reburied, or particular bones moved from one location to another — indicating continued active engagement with the remains of the dead.

These practices reflect broader patterns of ancestor veneration that are attested across many human societies and appear to have deep roots in the Neolithic period. The veneration of ancestors — the belief that deceased predecessors retain agency or influence in the world and deserve regular attention from the living — is one of the most widespread religious concepts in human cultures and likely has origins at least as deep as the Neolithic settlements of the Near East.
